data.title: Google to invest up to $40B in Anthropic, provide it with 5GW of computing power
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) said on Friday that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) is set to invest up to $40B in the generative artificial intelligence company and provide it with 5 gigawatts of computing power.

Alphabet shares jumped following the news.

Google is set to invest $10B initially into Anthropic at a $350B valuation, Bloomberg reported, which first broke the news, citing comments made by Anthropic. The other $30B would come if Anthropic hits certain milestones. 

Anthropic raised $30B in February that valued the company at $380B, post-funding.

As part of the deal, Google Cloud will give Anthropic 5 gigawatts worth of computing power over the next five years. Much of that will come in the form of Google's tensor processing units. The deal is an expansion of the announcement from earlier this month between Anthropic, Google, and Broadcom (AVGO).

Earlier this week, Amazon (AMZN), which, like Google, is a previous investor in Anthropic, said it was investing an additional $5B in Anthropic, also at a $350B valuation. It also has plans to add up to $20B more to that figure in the years ahead.

This is breaking news...

data.title: Erie targets Erie Secure Auto launch in four additional states this quarter as board chair transition takes effect
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Erie Indemnity Company (ERIE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Before we get into our first quarter results, I'd like to share some recent changes to the Erie Indemnity Company Board of Directors." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro) "Tom Hagen recently informed the Board of his decision to step down as Chairman after serving in the role for more than 20 years." (President & CEO NeCastro) "Following a special meeting of the Board of Directors on April 19, Jonathan Hirt Hagen was unanimously elected as Chairman of the Board." (President & CEO NeCastro) "Tom will continue to serve as a member of the Board as Chairman Emeritus and Chair of the Executive Committee." (President & CEO NeCastro)

  • "The Board of Directors also recently welcomed a new member, William Edwards, is an attorney and partner at Taft in Indianapolis, Indiana, where he practices employment law." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro) "Finally, we are deeply saddened by the recent passing of one of our long-time Board members and retired Erie executive, George Lucore." (President & CEO NeCastro)

  • "By the end of 2025 and now in the first quarter of 2026, we started to see a more balanced picture and early signs that we're beginning to turn a corner." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro) "We're still operating in a competitive market, and there's more work ahead, but the steady measured progress is encouraging." (President & CEO NeCastro)

  • "With significantly lower catastrophe and weather-related losses in the first quarter of 2026, the underwriting performance of the core business of the exchange continued to be more evident in contrast to the elevated weather activity we experienced a year ago." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski) "Following the period of significant rate increases across the industry, growth continues to be challenging." (Executive VP & CFO Pelkowski) "Higher premiums are impacting customer behavior and measures like policies in force and retention reflect a more competitive landscape." (Executive VP & CFO Pelkowski)

  • "Following a successful pilot in Ohio, we expanded into Virginia and West Virginia." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro) "We expect to introduce Erie Secure Auto in four additional states this quarter with continued expansion planned throughout the remainder of the year." (President & CEO NeCastro)

  • "In commercial lines, we're continuing to introduce Business Auto 2.0 across our footprint." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro) "We now have one remaining state, New York, to complete the rollout." (President & CEO NeCastro)

  • "Another rollout that will help connect our independent agents with customer leads is our new online quote platform." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro) "It was launched in Ohio in February, and we'll introduce it in Maryland, Pennsylvania, Virginia and West Virginia next month." (President & CEO NeCastro) "Over time, it will replace our existing online quoting tools." (President & CEO NeCastro)

  • "Today, more than half of our systems have been migrated to contemporary platforms, enhancing both the capabilities we deliver and the speed at which we bring new solutions to market." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro) "Over the past year, we've moved from early experimentation, scaled deployment of secure tools, including ChatGPT Enterprise now available across our employee population." (President & CEO NeCastro) "And to be clear, this isn't about replacing people, it's about helping our employees do their best work." (President & CEO NeCastro)

Outlook

  • No formal EPS, revenue, or combined ratio guidance was provided in the prepared remarks for Q1 2026.

  • "As we look ahead, our focus is on building on this momentum, continuing to move forward with discipline and staying grounded in the long-term approach that's guided us through this past year." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro)

  • "We expect to introduce Erie Secure Auto in four additional states this quarter with continued expansion planned throughout the remainder of the year." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro) "After rolling out to eight states in 2025, the product expanded to North Carolina, Virginia, Maryland and the District of Columbia in the first quarter of this year." (President & CEO NeCastro) "We now have one remaining state, New York, to complete the rollout." (President & CEO NeCastro)

  • "We'll introduce it in Maryland, Pennsylvania, Virginia and West Virginia next month." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro) "It's the result of several years of testing and refinement, and over time, it will replace our existing online quoting tools." (President & CEO NeCastro)

Financial Results

  • "Direct written premium grew 3.6% in the first quarter of 2026 compared to 13.9% in the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski) "While our average premium per policy grew 8.1% in the first quarter, policies in force were down 1.7% from this time last year and retention declined to 88%." (Executive VP & CFO Pelkowski)

  • "The Exchange's combined ratio was 99.4% in the first quarter of 2026 compared to 108.1% in the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski) "Non-catastrophe losses improved about 3 points compared to the prior year, reflective of stronger rate adequacy." (Executive VP & CFO Pelkowski) "From a catastrophe loss perspective, we saw an almost 7-point improvement from the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Pelkowski)

  • "Our policyholder surplus at the end of March was $10.1 billion, consistent with the December 2025 surplus level, reflecting essentially breakeven underwriting and investment results." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski)

  • "Net income was nearly $151 million or $2.88 per diluted share in the first quarter of 2026 compared to $138 million or $2.65 per diluted share in the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski) "Operating income increased approximately 10% to almost $167 million from $151 million in the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Pelkowski)

  • "Management fee revenue for policy issuance and renewal services grew approximately $31 million or 4.2%, in line with the increase in the direct written premiums of the Exchange, while we had more modest expense growth of 2.8% in the first quarter of 2026." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski)

  • "Commission expense, our largest cost of operations, increased 6.4% to $465 million, driven largely by agent incentive compensation due to the underwriting profitability improvement as well as higher base commissions driven by premium growth." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski) "Noncommission expenses decreased approximately 5.6% to $180 million, primarily driven by lower professional fees and expenses across most other categories, except for personnel costs, which were impacted by higher pension costs and increased compensation." (Executive VP & CFO Pelkowski)

  • "Our investment income in the first quarter was $22 million compared to $20 million in the same period of 2025, reflecting higher net investment income driven by higher yields and higher invested balances." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski) "For the first 3 months of 2026, our financial performance enabled us to pay our shareholders approximately $68 million in dividends." (Executive VP & CFO Pelkowski)

Q&A

  • No analyst Q&A session was included in this transcript. "This call was prerecorded, and there will be no question-and-answer session following the recording." (Operator)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Neutral. With no Q&A, there were no analyst concerns raised on the call. "There will be no question-and-answer session following the recording." (Operator)

  • Slightly positive. Management emphasized improvement versus last year’s weather-driven results while acknowledging competitive pressure: "we started to see a more balanced picture and early signs that we're beginning to turn a corner" (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro) and "growth continues to be challenging" (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski).

  • Compared with Q4 2025, Q1 2026 added governance and leadership transition specifics and leaned on operational rollout milestones; both quarters used similar caution about competition, with Q1 stating "there's more work ahead" (President & CEO NeCastro) alongside product expansion timelines.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 introduced board-level changes, including "Jonathan Hirt Hagen" being "unanimously elected as Chairman of the Board" and "William Edwards" joining the board, while Q4 2025 included the CEO’s succession timing: "I recently shared my intention to retire from Erie at the end of 2026." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro)

  • Q1 2026 stressed a slowdown in growth versus the prior-year quarter, with "direct written premium grew 3.6%" and "policies in force were down 1.7%," while Q4 2025 framed decelerating growth in the context of prior rate actions and a "continued slowdown in growth." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski)

  • Product and distribution initiatives remained a consistent theme, with Q1 detailing near-term rollout steps such as introducing the online quote platform to additional states "next month" and targeting Erie Secure Auto expansion to "four additional states this quarter," following Q4’s emphasis on Erie Secure Auto deployment plans and Business Auto 2.0 expansion.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We're still operating in a competitive market" and "growth continues to be challenging," with management attributing pressure to pricing dynamics and customer behavior: "Higher premiums are impacting customer behavior." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski)

  • "Given our pricing has reached more adequate levels, this has increased our competitive position challenge." (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski) "As these headwinds continue this year, we will respond through targeted pricing adjustments and product enhancements" was discussed in the prior quarter; in Q1, the mitigation focus was communicated through continued product rollouts and tools designed to "support growth, improving lead conversion and reducing connection time to our agents." (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as showing "early signs that we're beginning to turn a corner" (President & CEO Timothy NeCastro), citing lower catastrophe losses, a 99.4% combined ratio at the Exchange (Executive VP & CFO Julie Pelkowski), and higher Indemnity net income of nearly $151 million (Executive VP & CFO Pelkowski). The call also highlighted a board chair transition to Jonathan Hirt Hagen and near-term operational catalysts, including expanding Erie Secure Auto to four additional states this quarter and rolling out a new online quote platform to several states next month (President & CEO NeCastro), while reiterating that "growth continues to be challenging" in a competitive market (Executive VP & CFO Pelkowski).

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Orchid Island Capital outlines 15%-17% modeled return range while adding $108M and lifting leverage to 7.9%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Orchid Island Capital (ORC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "For the first quarter, we had a net loss of $0.11 per share compared to net income of $0.62 in Q4." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director George Haas)
  • "Our book value at 3/31 was $7.08 per share compared to $7.54 at December 31." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "Total return for the quarter was a negative 1.3% compared to 7.8% in Q4, and we declared dividends of $0.36 during both quarters." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "During Q1, we had an average balance of approximately $11 billion compared to $9.5 billion in Q4" and "our leverage ratio increased 7.9% compared to 7.4% at 12/31." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "Obviously, in this environment, the war headlines with respect to the war are driving performance of not just interest rates, but basically all risk assets." (Chairman, President & CEO Robert Cauley)
  • "All of these variables, stable interest rates, low swap yields and mortgage performance that's steady, all of these are very conducive for our business model" and "leaves us very bullish on the business model and levered MBS investing." (Chairman, President & CEO Cauley)
  • "We raised approximately $108 million in the quarter and an additional $28 million in early April" and "in total, we purchased approximately $1.6 billion of agency specified pools and TBAs with a focus on call protected collateral." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "At quarter end, approximately 92% of the portfolio was backed by specified pools with at least 10 ticks of payup." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "At present, we're currently funding in the 11 to 13 basis point range over SOFR" and "the hedge coverage is approximately 65% of our repo balance." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "Growth is accretive to earnings." (Chairman, President & CEO Cauley)

Outlook

  • "Looking forward, we expect speeds to ease in the coming months." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "Looking forward, while spreads have retraced from their recent wides, we continue to see an attractive environment for agency mortgages." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "At quarter end, the modeled returns for our combined portfolio, inclusive of hedges and at current funding levels were between 15% and 17% range return on equity." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "With respect to our outlook, the market is very appealing to us. Returns are still attractive." (Chairman, President & CEO Cauley)
  • "The big variable, of course, is the war. Nobody knows how that's going to play out." (Chairman, President & CEO Cauley)

Financial Results

  • "For the first quarter, we had a net loss of $0.11 per share" and "our book value at 3/31 was $7.08 per share." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "Our portfolio continued to grow" with "an average balance of approximately $11 billion" and "our liquidity at 3/31 was 54.5% compared to 57.7%." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "3-month CPR during the quarter was 14.7% compared to 15.7%." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "Speeds increased from 10.9 CPR in January to 16.3% CPR in March." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)

Q&A

  • Jason Weaver, JonesTrading Institutional Services, LLC, Research Division: "I noticed it looks like the effective duration of the portfolio extended a bit to about 3 as of 3/31. Was that intentional"? Chairman, President & CEO Cauley: "Yes, a little bit of both" and "we're trying to sort of not add too much hedge at the local highs." CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas: "we moved the average life of the hedge book out about 0.3 a year."
  • Jason Weaver, JonesTrading Institutional Services, LLC, Research Division: "can you talk about the sort of level of core spread income coverage..."? Chairman, President & CEO Cauley: "now when we appraise the current run rate, that's what drove us to move the dividend where it is" and "right now, the dividend yield is very much in line with what the portfolio is generating."
  • Mikhail Goberman, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC, Research Division: "Could you update us on current book value"? Chairman, President & CEO Cauley: "Book is up about 2.5% as of yesterday. We've given back some this week."
  • Mikhail Goberman, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC, Research Division: "Assuming rates on MBS continue to kind of creep up higher..."? CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas: "we expect to see a corresponding slowdown in prepay speeds" and "we'll probably buy more 5s and kind of first discount type coupons."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts slightly negative: Jason Weaver pressed on dividend sustainability ("it's obviously never easy to make the decision to make a cut"), and Mikhail Goberman focused on near-term book value sensitivity and portfolio construction.
  • Management slightly positive: Chairman, President & CEO Cauley emphasized that conditions were "very conducive" and said management was "quite bullish on the market going forward," while repeatedly framing uncertainty around "the big variable" being "the war."
  • Versus Q4, the prepared tone shifted from describing a benign backdrop to repeatedly referencing geopolitical shocks and spread volatility, while Q&A stayed focused on the same investor pressure points (book value, prepays, dividend).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "For the first quarter, we had a net loss of $0.11 per share compared to net income of $0.62 in Q4" alongside book value moving to "$7.08 per share compared to $7.54" and total return turning to "a negative 1.3% compared to 7.8% in Q4." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • Management’s market narrative moved from Q4’s "rates traded in a very tight range" (Chairman, President & CEO Cauley, Q4) to Q1’s focus that "the war headlines... are driving performance" and that spreads saw sharp moves after "geopolitical events in the Middle East" (Chairman, President & CEO Cauley; CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas, Q1).
  • Analysts’ emphasis was consistent, with both quarters featuring direct requests for "current book value" (Mikhail Goberman, Citizens JMP) and follow-ups on prepayments/dividend mechanics.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We could end up with stagflation." (Chairman, President & CEO Cauley)
  • "The big variable, of course, is the war. Nobody knows how that's going to play out." (Chairman, President & CEO Cauley)
  • "Prepayment speeds did pick up during the period" though "we expect speeds to ease in the coming months." (CFO, Chief Investment Officer, Secretary & Director Haas)
  • "Swap spreads have been moving more negative or tightening. That's bad for our hedges" while management highlighted mitigation via reallocating hedges, stating, "we took a lot of our hedges out of TBAs and put them into swaps." (Chairman, President & CEO Cauley)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 around a book value decline and a quarterly loss amid sharp, war-driven spread swings, while emphasizing portfolio liquidity, call protection, and improved funding. The company highlighted continued capital raising and deployment into specified pools, a hedge shift toward swaps, and an expectation for prepayment speeds to ease, with management pointing to modeled portfolio returns of 15% to 17% at quarter end while reiterating that the largest uncertainty remains the war and its market impact.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: The Bancorp forecasts $5.90 2026 EPS and $200M buybacks as credit sponsorship targets expand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Bancorp, Inc. (TBBK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Damian Kozlowski said the company was “maintaining our guidance of $5.90, EPS for '26 with $1.75 per share in the fourth quarter” and added: “Our expectation for '27 EPS is in a range $8.10 to $8.30.”

  • Kozlowski highlighted fintech momentum and new initiatives, saying “Fintech, GDV continues to grow above trend at 18% year-over-year,” and that “Cash at program has been launched. It will ramp up during '26 and '27 and show progressive accretion to our financials.” He also said, “As previously said, we expect to launch at least 2 significant additional programs in '26,” while “Embedded finance platform is close to completing the development of its first operational use case.”

  • Kozlowski tied capital return to the plan, saying “2026 buybacks are forecast to be $200 million total and $50 million a quarter in '26 with '27 buybacks equal to near 100% of net income in the year.”

  • Quote (Executive VP & CFO Dominic Canuso): “Ending loans for the quarter are $7.75 billion, which is a 9% non-annualized linked quarter growth and 22% growth year-over-year.”

Outlook

  • $1.75 Q4 2026 EPS target vs. $1.74 (analysts’ estimate for Q4 2026).

  • Kozlowski reiterated the annual outlook and framed timing for key drivers: “We have very little revenue for embedded finance in '26. We have more in '27,” and said embedded finance impact “will really be fulfilled in '27 and '28.”

  • On Cash App timing, Kozlowski said, “by the end of the year, it should be fairly meaningful to our financials,” and added: “we see a bump in the fourth quarter.”

  • Guidance language shifted on 2027 from a single point previously to a range now; Kozlowski said the 2027 expectation is “$8.10 to $8.30.”

Financial Results

  • Kozlowski reported profitability and growth metrics: “First quarter ROE was 35.1% and ROA was 2.57%,” and “Revenue growth in the quarter, which includes both fee and spread revenue was 15% year-over-year.”

  • Canuso described balance sheet mix shift and funding: “Credit sponsorship growth accounted for 88% of total loan growth linked quarter…bringing the segment to approximately 21% of total loans up from 15% prior quarter and 9% a year ago,” and “Average deposit cost of 1.7% in the quarter, which was a 7 basis point decrease from prior quarter.”

  • Canuso flagged margin pressure and how fee economics interact with NIM: “NIM was 3.87% in the quarter, down 43 basis points from prior quarter,” while “our fintech lending fees are the equivalent to an additional 24 basis points of net interest margin.”

  • Credit and reserves were discussed as improving: Canuso said “REBL criticized loans are down $24 million, or 29% to $59 million,” and “our traditional lending portfolio saw a provision reversal of $1.3 million.”

Q&A

  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: Asked about embedded finance timing and 2026 impact; CEO Kozlowski: “We have very little revenue for embedded finance in '26…that impact…will really be fulfilled in '27 and '28.”

  • Yanchunis, Raymond James: Asked how off-balance sheet deposits evolve; CEO Kozlowski: “It's both…we take the higher cost deposits off the balance sheet…we may get some spread on that…It's basically gravy…And they're volatile.”

  • Yanchunis, Raymond James: Asked about the Aubrey sale and use of proceeds; CFO Canuso: “We expect the property to be operating breakeven by the end of this quarter,” and said the bank is aiming for “a stabilized valuation,” while CEO Kozlowski discussed stabilization plans and said appraisals were “in the low 50s.”

  • Yanchunis, Raymond James: Asked how much of the balance sheet could be dedicated to credit sponsorship; CEO Kozlowski: “we might -- originally, we were thinking 10%. And then we thought more like 30% or 40% of the balance sheet possibly in the next 3 to 4 years.”

  • Grant (for Manuel Navas), Piper Sandler: Asked about fintech loan loss reserve shift; CFO Canuso: “the secured product did outperform the growth…there was a mix shift towards that product.”

  • Grant (for Manuel Navas), Piper Sandler: Asked pace toward $2B fintech loan goal and effects; CFO Canuso: “It does not change our full year targets or expectations…there was just a little bit of a pull forward of volume.”

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: Asked whether Cash App contributed to GDV; CEO Kozlowski: “No, very little.”

  • Switzer, KBW: Pressed on fintech loan economics and velocity/holding periods; CEO Kozlowski said loans may be held “from 3 to 30 days…Usually, it's only a few days,” and added, “We intend to securitize a lot of it.”

  • Switzer, KBW: Asked about proposed citizenship-information executive order; CEO Kozlowski: “we can't really comment on it,” while noting “we do collect a lot of…information like social security numbers…for many of our” accounts depending on type.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, focusing on timing risk and economics, including “partner delays are a thing in this space” (Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James) and detailed pressure-testing of fintech lending yields and NIM (Timothy Switzer, KBW).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing timing and uncertainty, including “very little revenue…in '26” for embedded finance (CEO Kozlowski) and describing off-balance-sheet deposit economics as “gravy” and “volatile” (CEO Kozlowski).

  • Versus the prior quarter, the Q&A stayed centered on fintech ramp timing, but moved deeper into balance sheet allocation, loan velocity/securitization mechanics, and potential regulatory operational burden.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Kozlowski maintained the 2026 EPS guidance at $5.90, while shifting 2027 framing from “$8.25 a share” previously to “a range $8.10 to $8.30” now.

  • Credit sponsorship balances accelerated from “$1.1 billion” at Q4 2025 to “$1.65 billion” in Q1 2026, with Kozlowski calling the increase “a 50% nonannualized increase over the fourth quarter of '25.”

  • Off-balance sheet deposits expanded from “$400 million” (Q4 2025 discussion) to “$1.34 billion” (Q1 2026), with Canuso pointing to “continued growth of our partnership-based deposit franchise.”

  • NIM expectations previously contemplated compression “near 4%” (CFO Canuso in Q4), while Q1 reported NIM at 3.87% and management spent more time explaining fee-equivalent margin components.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management repeatedly flagged fintech execution timing risk, with Kozlowski saying announcements are “subject to our partners' marketing time lines” and embedded finance “does take a while to fully build out the capability.”

  • Canuso highlighted NIM sensitivity to mix and rate dynamics: “The decrease versus prior quarter is driven by both the mix shift in loans to credit sponsorship and the lagged impact of the lower short-term rates on variable rate loans.”

  • The Aubrey remains an operational focus and potential timing variable; Canuso said it is expected to be “operating breakeven by the end of this quarter,” while Kozlowski described a multi-phase reconditioning plan.

Final Takeaway

Management reiterated $5.90 EPS guidance for 2026 and a $1.75 Q4 2026 EPS target while describing fintech-driven balance sheet repositioning toward credit sponsorship loans, larger off-balance sheet deposit flows, and continued buybacks (forecast at $200M in 2026). In Q&A, executives repeatedly emphasized that embedded finance contributes “very little revenue” in 2026, with the main financial ramp tied to credit sponsorship growth, Cash App scaling through 2026 and into 2027, and continued execution on partner-dependent timelines.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wisconsin DOJ sues Kalshi, Robinhood, Polymarket, Coinbase, Crypto.com over sports betting
data.description:
The Wisconsin Department of Justice has filed lawsuits against Kalshi (KALSHI), Robinhood (HOOD), Polymarket (POLYMARKET), Coinbase (COIN), and Crypto.com over illegal sports betting.

The state department alleges that the companies flouted the laws by disguising sports betting on their online platforms as "event contracts".

Furthermore, the complaint alleges that the accused firms collect a fee for every bet made, thereby generating revenue by violating the state's gambling laws.

"Thinly disguising unlawful conduct doesn't make it lawful," said AG Kaul. "These companies' alleged facilitation of sports betting in Wisconsin should be shut down."

The lawsuits, requiring preliminary and permanent injunctions, were filed in Dane County on Thursday.

Wisconsin is the latest state to take action against prediction markets over sports-related event contracts.

Currently, the U.S. prediction market is sitting in a rapidly evolving but still unsettled regulatory environment. The CFTC has moved toward a formal framework, including a March 12 advance notice of proposed rulemaking and a staff advisory, while also withdrawing an earlier proposal that could have tightened limits on sports- and politics-related contracts.

At the same time, state regulators and litigants are aggressively challenging the industry, especially where contracts overlap with gambling or sports betting. The result today is a patchwork of federal oversight, active rulemaking, and ongoing state-level legal pressure.

Earlier this month, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission filed a lawsuit against three states—Arizona, Connecticut, and Illinois—over unlawful efforts to regulate prediction markets.

data.title: Amerant projects 2Q 2026 deposits of $8B and targets a ~60% efficiency ratio as Venezuela-driven inflows build
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Amerant Bancorp (AMTB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “I’m proud of the continued progress we have made on the 3 priorities we outlined last quarter, stabilizing the business, optimizing our credit portfolio and growing sustainably.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Carlos Iafigliola)

  • “We exited and transferred to held for sale another group of loans that we no longer consider core to our business,” and the company “fundamentally shifted underwriting, prioritizing borrowers with proven stable operating history over projection-based lending” while “moving away from out-of-market collateral projects, except selectively for existing clients in core markets.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • “Our net income for Q1 was in line with our guidance,” and management said it “significantly reduced noninterest expenses quarter-over-quarter, supported by better-than-expected cost savings,” adding, “our expense management efforts represents approximately $30 million in cost savings for 2026.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • “This quarter, we delivered, recording $188 million of total deposit growth in Q1, from which $95 million came from Venezuela,” and management described these as “quite attractive due to their stability, overall cost of funds and beta in rates-up cycle.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • “Going forward, we will be discussing results without breaking down core versus noncore metrics in our financials,” adding, “we would like to be more selective with adjustments with the goal of providing a clearer and more straightforward view of our quarterly performance.” (Senior EVP & CFO Sharymar Yepez)

  • “We redesigned our annual review format to drive deeper risk identification and recalibrated the review threshold from total credit exposures of $5 million to $3 million,” and “expect to review all exposures over $1 million through our standardized review,” alongside “quarterly top 20 reviews across CRE, C&I and Private Banking segments.” (Chief Credit Officer & Senior EVP Lee Cragg)

Outlook

  • “For 2Q '26, we project loan balances to reach approximately $7 billion,” and “for the full year 2026, we expect annualized loan growth of approximately 7%,” with growth governed by “2 deliberate and nonnegotiable priorities,” including continuing to “exit certain credits” and pursuing growth “consistent with our risk appetite.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • “We expect deposits to reach $8 billion by 2Q '26 and cumulative deposit growth between 8% to 10% for 2026,” and said that confidence is “supported by emerging opportunities in Venezuela.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • “We expect net interest margin to be in the 3.4% to 3.5% range in 2Q '26, stabilizing around 3.4% towards year-end,” and projected “approximately $68 million to $69 million in expenses for 2Q '26” with “quarterly expenses stabilizing around $68 million by the second part of the year” as the bank works “towards our target efficiency ratio of approximately 60%.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • Compared with Q4 2025 guidance language, management moved from projecting 1Q 2026 net interest margin of “3.65% to 3.70%” and expenses “$70 million to $71 million in the first half of 2026” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, Q4 call) to a lower 2Q 2026 NIM range and a tighter expense run-rate centered on ~$68 million. (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez, Q1 call)

Financial Results

  • “Diluted income per share for the first quarter was $0.44 compared to $0.07 in the fourth quarter,” while “net interest income was $80.3 million, down $9.9 million from $90.2 million in the fourth quarter,” and “the contraction of our financial margin” was “to 3.55% from 3.78%.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • “Noninterest expense was $66.9 million, down by $39.9 million or 37.3% from $106.8 million,” and management cited cost actions including “$3.3 million savings in vendor contract renegotiations,” partially offset by “$1.7 million in an impairment on investment carried at cost and $1.8 million in net losses on loans held for sale.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • Capital actions continued: “CET1 remains strong at 11.84%,” and during Q1 the bank “repurchased 859,493 shares at a weighted average price of $21.77” and paid a “$0.09 per share” dividend, with the board approving another “$0.09 per share payable on May 29.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • Credit metrics and exits were a central focus: “Nonperforming loans were up $4.7 million or 2.7% for a total of $176.1 million,” and the company reported “loan payoffs totaling $59.5 million and loans sold totaling $65.7 million during the period.” (Chief Credit Officer & Senior EVP Cragg)

Q&A

  • Evan Yee, Raymond James: asked why expenses ran better than expected and how durable savings are; Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola said the bank “accelerated some of the contract renegotiation,” and Senior EVP & CFO Yepez said the goal is “sustainability quarter-over-quarter,” while also expecting investments in “people and technology” as the bank plans to “cross the $10 billion threshold.”

  • Evan Yee, Raymond James: asked about buyback appetite; Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola said “the leftover of the buyback right now is $21 million” and management is “planning to complete the buyback through Q2.”

  • Nicholas Lorenzoni, Stephens: asked if the mid-2Q CRE exit “wrap[s] up the heavy lifting on derisking”; Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola said the next phase is “a risk calibration exercise,” with “another exit up coming weeks,” leaving “$130 million in available for sale,” and said “the plan is to continue to execute” to build “a portfolio that is more granular.”

  • Wood Lay, KBW: questioned why NIM came in below guidance and whether safer production is lower yielding; Senior EVP & CFO Yepez cited “a different asset mix,” “timing of the funding of the loan growth,” and that new production aligned with risk appetite is expected to come “with an overall lower yield,” while noting “I didn’t see anything significant this quarter” on nonaccrual impacts.

  • Wood Lay, KBW: asked how the bank is scaling Venezuela and what deposit costs look like; Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola said the bank is “looking to increase the staff,” described “a path towards reducing the number of sanctions,” and said international deposit costs are “around 1.30% actually even a little bit lower 1.15%,” while “the incremental deposits that we’re getting are actually sub 1%.”

  • Wood Lay, KBW: asked about charge-off expectations amid geopolitical/inflation concerns; Senior EVP & CFO Yepez said, “we have no direct exposure to exploration or extraction on the oil piece,” and guided to charge-offs of “around 30 to 35 basis points.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on execution details (expenses durability, NIM under-guidance, remaining derisking) with questions like “does that effectively wrap up the heavy lifting on derisking?” (Nicholas Lorenzoni, Stephens) and “It came below the guide you all had given for the quarter.” (Wood Lay, KBW)

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and process-driven in prepared remarks (e.g., “strong execution and tangible progress” and “remain confident”), and more explanatory in Q&A with bridges and caveats such as “we’re carefully assessing right now” on margin sensitivity to international deposits. (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • Versus Q4 2025, management used more definitive execution language on costs and Venezuela-related deposit inflows in Q1 2026, while Q4 framed Venezuela as early-stage: “It’s still too soon to understand the impact.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola, Q4 call)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized realized Venezuela-linked deposit inflows and cross-sell into wealth, while Q4 2025 positioned Venezuela as an “opportunity” tied to reopening dynamics; Q1 quantified that “$95 million came from Venezuela” out of “$188 million of total deposit growth.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • Expense narrative shifted from restructuring-driven noise in Q4 (including “contract termination costs” and “staff separation costs”) to a stabilized run-rate in Q1: “the run-up rate seems to be closer to the $68 million for the entire year quarter-over-quarter.” (Senior EVP, Interim CEO & Director Iafigliola)

  • Credit work progressed from broad review coverage in Q4 to tighter monitoring and ongoing exits in Q1, including new portfolio-management staffing and lower review thresholds, alongside continued loan sales/payoffs and projections for additional CRE exits. (Chief Credit Officer & Senior EVP Cragg)

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged that near-term profitability can be pressured by credit exits and NPLs: “While this may have a short-term impact, it improves the long-term sustainability of our business.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • Funding and margin risk remained tied to rate and deposit repricing timing: “There’s still uncertainty as to the timing of the repricing of those deposits,” which management said “will impact the guidance to the NIM.” (Senior EVP & CFO Yepez)

  • Credit resolution execution risk persisted alongside downgrades: NPLs rose to “$176.1 million,” and management described continued work on “restructures or obtaining additive credit enhancements where possible.” (Chief Credit Officer & Senior EVP Cragg)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of executing its stabilization plan through lower expenses, continued credit exits and upgraded monitoring, and a notable step-up in international deposits tied to Venezuela. For Q2 and full-year 2026, the company guided to loan growth and deposit growth targets while projecting net interest margin to settle in the mid-3% range and quarterly expenses to stabilize near $68 million as it works toward an approximately 60% efficiency ratio, alongside ongoing share repurchases and dividends.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Zoom should put itself up for sale if it fails to drive up shareholder value: Spruce Point
data.description:
Spruce Point Management suggests the digital communication platform Zoom (ZM) should place itself on the auction block if it fails to drive up meaningful shareholder value within the next year.

However, the firm believes the company has upside potential, and several remedies can be applied before taking such a drastic measure.

"After conducting a forensic review of Zoom Communications, Inc., we see material upside potential in the Company's shares," Spruce Point said. "Zoom is the leader in video communications and was a COVID-19 pandemic darling. However, a growth slowdown, persistent skepticism regarding its market opportunity and competitive position, and the current SaaS-pocalypse have, we believe unfairly, driven Zoom's valuation to egregiously low levels, particularly considering the Company’s estimated $1.2 billion stake in AI leader Anthropic (ANTHRO)."

To be fair, Zoom shares have not suffered the same fate as most of the software sector has recently. It is up 6% year to date, while the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) has declined 20% over that time frame.

To accelerate shareholder value further, Spruce Point suggested several recommendations for the company. These include improving capital allocation, reallocating and reducing operating expenses, restructuring and refocusing the international business, and improving marketing. Additional remedies recommended are avoiding M&A mistakes, consolidating insider selling, collapsing its dual-class share structure, and lobbying for inclusion in the S&P 500 (SP500).

If all these fail, Spruce Point said the company should put itself up for sale.

"We believe Zoom should pursue a sale transaction if management cannot drive shareholder value creation within a year," Spruce Point added. "We view Zoom as a highly attractive acquisition target and believe there is a broad universe of potential strategic buyers with massive financial wherewithal. Our analysis of the largest software buyouts of the past five years suggests a 33% premium from private equity could set an M&A valuation floor."

data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq rise, Dow lags after Israel-Lebanon ceasefire extension
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Friday as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.7%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.5%. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.1%.

Intel (INTC) shares jumped 22.6% after its Q1 results delivered a beat, and now its second-quarter revenue outlook is expected to surpass what analysts anticipated. Other semiconductors also rose, including Nvidia (NVDA), TSMC (TSM), AMD (AMD), Arm Holdings (ARM), and Qualcomm (QCOM).

Treasury yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points lower at 4.31%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 6 basis points to 3.79%.

U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting ambassadors of both countries in Washington. 

"The meeting went very well! The United States is going to work with Lebanon in order to help it protect itself from Hezbollah. The ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended by THREE WEEKS," he said via Truth Social.

Trump also ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz, adding that there will be "no hesitation."

Moreover, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps navy laid more mines in the Strait of Hormuz this week, according to a report by Axios.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were slightly lower at $93, while Brent (CO1:COM) was last around $104 per barrel.

On the economy, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April—to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March—according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers.

data.title: Kinsale signals focus on sustaining "low 20s" ROE as commercial property competition persists
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kinsale Capital Group (KNSL) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "In the first quarter 2026, Kinsale's diluted operating earnings per share increased by 37.7% over the first quarter of 2025, generating an annualized operating return on equity of 24%." (Chairman of the Board, CEO & President Michael Kehoe)

  • "Gross written premium was down 0.5%, but net written premium grew by 5.6% for the quarter" and "Kinsale's combined ratio was 77.4%." (CEO Kehoe)

  • "Much of the headwind to our growth emanates from our large commercial property division" with "an abundance of competition and falling rates," while "excluding the Commercial Property division, Kinsale's growth in gross written premium was 6% for the first quarter." (CEO Kehoe)

  • "In the first quarter, new business submissions were up 6%. New business quotes were up 8% and new business bind orders were up 9%." (CEO Kehoe)

  • "Our average policy premium for the quarter... was $12,200 per policy, down from $14,200" in Q1 2025, which management tied to a pivot toward smaller transactions where "margins continue to be robust." (CEO Kehoe)

  • "Net income and net operating earnings" increased "by 26.1% and 36.3%, respectively, quarter-over-quarter," and "diluted earnings per share continues to improve and was $5.11 per share" in Q1. (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Bryan Petrucelli)

  • "The 77.4% combined ratio for the quarter included 4.5 points from net favorable prior year loss reserve development" and "less than 1 point in cat losses this year compared to 6 points in Q1 last year." (CFO Petrucelli)

  • "We produced a 21.1% expense ratio for the quarter compared to 20% last year" while "the other underwriting expense" ratio was "10.3%" vs. "10.5%" in Q1 2025; the expense ratio increase was attributed to "a higher net commission ratio resulting from higher reinsurance retentions." (CFO Petrucelli)

  • "We have noticed more aggressive competition in some long tail lines like construction over the last quarter," while small business property and multiple casualty divisions "experienced favorable underwriting conditions and strong growth." (Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Stuart Winston)

Outlook

  • No formal EPS, revenue, or premium guidance was provided in this transcript; management described operating posture as "our continued focus on smaller transactions where margins continue to be robust" in a market where "E&S market conditions... continued to be competitive." (CEO Kehoe)

  • On profitability thresholds, management reiterated, "we always manage all of our product lines to a low 20s return on equity or greater" and added, "I wouldn't expect a meaningful deterioration" from the quarter’s "24%" ROE. (CEO Kehoe)

  • On reinsurance, management said it reviews retentions annually: "we'll look at it again this year, but I can't really commit at this moment to how the treaties will be placed" and noted "it's a 6/1 renewal date." (CEO Kehoe)

Financial results

  • "Diluted earnings per share... was $5.11 per share for the quarter compared to $3.71 per share" in Q1 2025. (CFO Petrucelli)

  • Investment and float were positioned as ongoing earnings levers: "net investment income increased by 26.5%" year-over-year in Q1, and "Kinsale's float... grew to $3.3 billion at March 31 from $3.1 billion at the end of 2025." (CFO Petrucelli)

  • Portfolio yield context remained similar: "Annual gross return was 4.5%" and "new money yields are averaging around 5%, with an average duration slightly above 4 years" on fixed maturities. (CFO Petrucelli)

Q&A

  • Daniel Cohen, BMO Capital Markets: Asked about quote/bind KPI volatility and whether to accept higher loss ratios for growth; Chairman of the Board, CEO & President Michael Kehoe said, "I wouldn't overthink" short-period movements and added, "we always manage all of our product lines to a low 20s return on equity or greater" and "I wouldn't expect a meaningful deterioration" from the quarter’s ROE.

  • Hristian Getsov, Wells Fargo: Pressed on whether accident-year loss ratio benefited from one-offs; Chief Actuary Salmaan Allibhai responded, "There's nothing out of the ordinary, no one-time adjustments" and cited seasonality where "the first quarter is a little bit higher." Getsov also asked about E&S homeowners down 22%; Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Stuart Winston said, "The high-value market is experiencing some increased competition" and "the average premium is dropping a little bit." Getsov asked about reinsurance amid a below-average hurricane forecast; CEO Kehoe said, "I can't really commit" on placement decisions ahead of the "6/1 renewal date."

  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies: Asked about shifting casualty competition; Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Winston said competition from "fronts and MGAs and new companies" is increasing "specifically in construction" over "the last 4 to 5 months," while he added, "Anything related to auto, we're seeing meaningful opportunities there." On submissions, CEO Kehoe said ex-commercial property submissions were "up... 9%" and described the environment as "reasonably steady."

  • Pablo Singzon, JPMorgan: Asked about retention and whether tech-enabled competitors are pressing small business; Winston said, "New business hit ratios and renewal hit ratios have been consistent quarter-to-quarter for a long time." CEO Kehoe said on competitors’ tech efforts, "No," adding Kinsale can adopt AI faster because "we don't have legacy software."

  • Mark Hughes, Truist: Asked if property pricing is still drifting down; CEO Kehoe said, "We don't really have any good news to report there" and pointed to easier comps in the "second half of the year." Hughes asked about equity portfolio performance; CEO Kehoe said, "It's performing well" and noted it has "lagged the S&P" since late 2022 while matching the "Vanguard VYM." Hughes asked about cash from operations; CFO Petrucelli said cash flow could be viewed as "trending it with net written premiums."

  • Rowland Mayor, RBC: Asked for commercial property mix specifics; Winston clarified, "That commercial property division, that is the large shared and layered division." Kehoe said average premium in that division is "between $30,000 and $40,000 per policy" and added the prior average "might have been north of $50,000," citing a shift to smaller accounts.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on growth headwinds and sustainability questions, including "We don't really have any good news to report" on property competition and whether Kinsale would "deteriorate" loss ratios for growth. (CEO Kehoe; Daniel Cohen, BMO Capital Markets)

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks, emphasizing resilience: "we continue to have a high level of confidence in our model" and "we remain very confident in the long-term strength and durability of the platform," while Q&A responses were more cautious on property and reinsurance specifics: "I can't really commit at this moment." (Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Winston; CEO Kehoe)

  • Versus Q4 2025, management continued to frame competition as intense in large commercial property, but Q1 carried more explicit acknowledgment on property: "We don't really have any good news to report there," compared with prior-quarter stabilization language that "at some point... it should stabilize." (CEO Kehoe, Q1 2026; CEO Kehoe, Q4 2025)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1’s growth narrative leaned more heavily on mix shift to smaller policies ("$12,200 per policy") and added quarterly divisional disclosure, while Q4 emphasized capital return actions including "the $250 million buyback authorization" and a dividend increase to "$0.25" from "$0.17." (CEO Kehoe, Q1 2026; CEO Kehoe, Q4 2025)

  • Pricing commentary showed continued softening in the composite: Amwins index rate change of "a rate decrease of 3 1/3%" in Q1 vs. "a rate decrease of 2.7%" in Q4. (Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Winston, Q1 2026; Winston, Q4 2025)

  • Profitability remained strong but the combined ratio moved from "71.7%" in Q4 to "77.4%" in Q1, with both quarters benefiting from "less than 1 point" of cat losses while Q1 referenced last year’s Q1 cat load of "6 points." (CFO Petrucelli, Q4 2025; CFO Petrucelli, Q1 2026)

Risks and concerns

  • Large commercial property remained the clearest growth and pricing pressure point: "an abundance of competition and falling rates" and "We don't really have any good news to report there." (CEO Kehoe)

  • Competitive intensity broadened in selected casualty areas: "more aggressive competition" in construction from "fronts and MGAs and new companies." (Executive VP & Chief Underwriting Officer Winston)

  • Management highlighted uncertainty around reinsurance structure ahead of renewal: "I can't really commit at this moment" on retention and treaty placement decisions. (CEO Kehoe)

Final takeaway

Kinsale management described Q1 2026 as a quarter where profitability remained strong alongside a deliberate shift toward smaller accounts, with growth constrained mainly by intensified competition and falling rates in large shared-and-layered commercial property. Executives reiterated a "low 20s" ROE operating target, pointed to continued momentum in submissions/quotes/binds outside commercial property, and emphasized that technology and AI adoption—enabled by an enterprise system without legacy software—remains central to efficiency and underwriting execution as market competition persists into the mid-year reinsurance renewal.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
